title: Eschatology: The Nature and Identity of the Church — Part 1
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Lesson 6 on Eschatology_ The Nature and Identity of the Church - Part 1.txt

outline.title: Why the Nature of the Church Matters for Eschatology
subpoints: The identity of the church is the core difference between historic premillennialism and dispensational premillennialism
Dispensationalism's sine qua non (Ryrie): dispensationalists keep Israel and the church distinct
Historic premillennialism is born from covenant theology — the church is tied to the Abrahamic covenant
Chafer's summary: God pursues two distinct purposes — earthly people (Judaism) and heavenly people (Christianity) — a complete separation
Ryrie: 'A man who fails to distinguish Israel and the church will inevitably not hold to dispensational distinctions'
The connection between the church and the Abrahamic covenant determines how we interpret Old Testament prophecy about Israel

outline.title: Dispensationalism — Definition and Origins
subpoints: Dispensationalism was formalized in England by J.N. Darby in the 1800s; popularized in the US through prophetic conferences in the Niagara area
The Scofield Reference Bible brought dispensationalism into American evangelical homes
Original teaching: God saved people differently in different 'dispensations' (epochs) — by faith in a vague higher power, by law-keeping, etc.
This has been largely abandoned; modern dispensationalists (e.g., MacArthur) affirm that OT saints were saved by faith
Core distinction: OT promises to Israel are unconditional — every promise must be literally fulfilled for ethnic Israel
God put a 'pause' on Israel's kingdom promises when Israel rejected Christ; the church is plan B, a parenthesis
Future tribulation is only for ethnic Israel; the church will be raptured out beforehand
In the millennium: ethnic Israel in the earthly kingdom, the church as the heavenly people — Christ effectively has two brides

outline.title: Six Ways the Word Church Is Used in the Bible
subpoints: The collection of all true believers through the ages — the invisible church (Hebrews 12:22-23; 'ecclesia' in the Septuagint for OT Israel)
The collection of professing Christians and their children at a given time — the visible church (1 Corinthians 10:32)
The gathering of believers in the same place — the local church (Colossians 4:15)
The collection of several local churches in a geographical area — similar to a presbytery (Acts 9:31)
The church as an organism, emphasizing the people in it — the body metaphor (1 Corinthians 12-14)
The church as an institution — given the sacraments, exercising discipline, called the pillar and ground of the truth (1 Timothy 3:15)
The sixth use — the institutional church — is the one most relevant to discussing the church's origin and marks

outline.title: The Three Marks of a True Church (Belgic Confession, Article 29)
subpoints: The Belgic Confession is the only Reformed confession whose author died as a martyr for the faith — Guido de Bres
Mark 1: the pure doctrine of the gospel is preached
Mark 2: the sacraments are administered faithfully as Christ instituted them
Mark 3: church discipline is exercised in the punishment of sin
Calvin disagreed that discipline is of the essence of the church; but the result is practically the same — a church without discipline will die
With these three marks as the definition, we can identify the first time in the Bible when they all appear together

outline.title: The Church Began with Abraham — Genesis 17
subpoints: The first time all three marks (preaching, sacraments, discipline) appear together is in the Abrahamic covenant — Genesis 17
Genesis 17:9-13: circumcision commanded as a sign and seal of the covenant — a sacrament (Romans 4 calls it this)
Genesis 17:14: anyone who refuses circumcision is cut off — church discipline from the very beginning
Genesis 18:19: God says He knows Abraham will command his children to keep the way of the Lord — preaching within the household
John 8: Jesus says Abraham knew of Him and rejoiced; Romans 4: Abraham believed the same gospel we believe
Therefore the church began with Abraham in Genesis, not in Acts 2 — American evangelicalism is off by a few thousand years
People were calling on the name of the Lord in Genesis 4, but organized, regulated corporate worship begins with Abraham

summary:
This sixth lesson begins a multi-part study of the nature and identity of the church, which Pastor Tito Lyro argues is the most important doctrinal issue for understanding eschatology. The core of the difference between historic premillennialism (the church's position) and dispensational premillennialism is whether Israel and the church are the same entity or two entirely separate peoples of God. The pastor quotes Charles Ryrie and Lewis Sperry Chafer to show that dispensationalism's defining feature is the absolute separation of Israel and the church.

Dispensationalism, formalized by J.N. Darby in England and popularized in the US through prophetic conferences and the Scofield Reference Bible, teaches that God pursues two distinct purposes: an earthly kingdom for ethnic Israel and a heavenly people called the church. Original dispensationalism taught different modes of salvation in different eras, but modern proponents have abandoned that in favor of affirming faith-based salvation throughout redemptive history. The irreducible core remains the distinction: all OT promises to Israel must be literally fulfilled for ethnic Israel; the church is parenthetical — 'plan B' after Israel rejected Christ. This leads directly to a pre-tribulation rapture and two resurrections into separate eternal destinies.

The pastor then surveys the six ways the New Testament uses the word 'church,' arguing that the sense most relevant to the origin of the church is the institutional church — the body to which sacraments were given, which exercises discipline, and which is called the pillar and ground of the truth (1 Timothy 3:15). Using the three marks of a true church from the Belgic Confession (preaching, sacraments, discipline), he traces the first appearance of all three marks to the Abrahamic covenant in Genesis 17: circumcision is commanded as the sacrament (sign and seal, per Romans 4), anyone refusing it is cut off (discipline), and Abraham is charged to command his household in the way of the Lord (preaching). The lesson closes with the bold assertion that the church began with Abraham in Genesis — several thousand years before the Pentecost of Acts 2.
transcript:
## Introduction

We are starting a study in the subject of what happens in the future. And we've looked at it from the perspective that the future is from the moment of our death on. And we've accomplished some of those things as far as looking at them. And the theological name for what we study is eschatology.

For the last two weeks we have been trying to define the different views that have been formulated that have been accepted as Orthodox positions as right positions in the reformed church. And so far we've focused on the difference between those views. We've looked at what Amillennialists believe. We looked at what post-millennialists believe and what historic premillennialists believed. Now today and likely next week or two we'll look at a doctrine that all three positions have in common and that will distinguish these three positions from a fourth position of a dispensationalism that we'll consider today a little bit.

Now let's think for a just a moment the things that are in common among the three different positions that were already studied. What is that the Amillennialists, post-millennialists and historic premillennialists have in common? Well they all believe in a physical future return of Christ. They all believe in a physical future resurrection of the saints. They all have that in common. They also all believe in a future physical resurrection of the wicked. They all believe in a judgment to come for the wicked. And they all believe that Christ will be ultimately declared victorious and he will rule in the eternity future.

So these things are in common with all those positions. And it is not a biblical position if they deny any of those things. There are some that believe that every single prophecy in the Bible has already been fulfilled. And this is it. So this is heaven we are good to go from now on. So that's not a acceptable biblical position.

The doctrine that we will be considering today and in the next week or two is the nature and identity of the church. This is a doctrine of major importance. And the reason I want to talk about the nature and the identity of the church in a series on eschatology is because the doctrine of the church is usually follows under a different category of the church. But we have to be careful that we don't isolate different areas so much that we don't see contact or connections between them. And the reason I wanted to talk about it particularly as we study historic premillennialism, which is the position I think the Bible teaches, I want to dissipate the founded accusation that historic premillennialism is the same thing as dispensational premillennialism. And I'm going to define that word.

The truth is that there is a world of difference between these two systems, and the core of that difference is the identity of the church. A historic premillennialism is born out of a covenantal understanding of the scriptures that ties the church with the Abrahamic covenant. That ties who we are today with the promises made to Abraham back in Genesis 12, 13, 15, 17, and 22. And this is anything but dispensationalism. So please listen to me, get over the idea that in order to be a historic premillennialist, you have to be a dispensationalist. It's a strawman argument that's not true.

## Dispensationalism — Definition

Charles Ryrie, which some of you know, some of you still carry his study Bible around. Charles Ryrie, who is considered the father of modern dispensationalism. Charles Ryrie is the famous professor at Dallas Theological Seminary. He says the following concerning dispensationalism in his book, Dispensationalism Today:

What then is the sine qua non of dispensationalism? What is it that is the essence — if you don't have it, you don't have dispensationalism anymore? The answer is three fold. Dispensationalists keep Israel and the church distinct. Chafer summarizes it as follows: The dispensationalist believes that throughout the ages, God is pursuing two distinct purposes. One related to the earth with earthly people and earthly objectives involved, which is Judaism, while the other is related to heaven with heavenly people and heavenly objectives involved, which is Christianity.

So this teaches that God has two people — a heavenly spiritual people that we call the church and a completely separate, no connection, earthly people called Israel. And ethnic Israel, that's what they say is living today. Ryrie continues: this is probably the most basic theological test of whether or not a man is a dispensationalist, and it is undoubtedly the most practical and conclusive. A man who fails to distinguish Israel and the church will inevitably not hold to dispensational distinctions.

So that's really at the bottom line. That's what we're going to talk about, the nature of the church, because that's really the major difference between what I'm trying to teach you and dispensationalism. Now Ryrie says there are two other distinctions: dispensationalists interpret scripture literally, and dispensationalists see the goal of life as the glory of God. Now these two other points are straw men. Every position claims to interpret the scriptures literally because interpreting something literally means interpreting how it was meant to be interpreted, taking into account figures of speech, taking account of the intention of the author. And we also agree with the glory of God. The first question of the most Reformed catechism ever written asks, what is the chief end of man? To glorify God and to enjoy him forever. Everything exists. We exist to glorify God. So Ryrie may have gotten those two wrong. But the first one is completely right — that is the difference.

Dispensationalism teaches that OT promises to Israel are conceived of as unconditional — every single promise made to Israel has to happen. There is a greater emphasis on the physical nature of these promises and on their literal fulfillment. And they say that in the time of Jesus the nation of Israel rejected the Messiah. And the difference they have is that because of that, that was a pause there — the kingdom promises were thus withdrawn from Israel until the millennium when they will be fulfilled. As if God put a pause on that promise and now he's going to run plan B on the side and that plan B is called the church. Secondary action, even though that's supposed to be the bride of Christ, but that's secondary.

And then all future prophecies regarding the millennium are only to ethnic Israel, nothing from the church there. They make a big deal about the 70 weeks of Daniel. The 69 weeks have already happened and we're waiting for the last week, and that last week is going to be all about ethnic or physical Israel. The future tribulation will happen to judge and purify physical ethnic Israel. The New Testament church is a separate body from Israel with separate functions, promises and destination. Future Israel is never going to go to heaven — it's going to be an earthly existence. In essence Christ has two brides. The earthly bride called Israel and the heavenly bride called the church. In essence Christ is a bigamist with different brides, the earthly bride and this heavenly bride.

And they also teach that at the end of the millennium Israel will have been converted and will receive the kingdom promises, which promises will include a reestablishment of the Jewish legal law system and temple sacrifices as well. Earthly Israel will be an earthly people. The church a heavenly people. Israel and the church are entirely distinct. Future tribulation will be only for Israel, not the church.

This is a position I assume that a lot of you have been most exposed to in the United States because it is a prevailing position of evangelicalism today. A lot of times adopted thoughtlessly — there's not a lot of thinking about it — but it's not a biblical position.

## Six Ways the Word Church Is Used in the Bible

So my goal next is to actually look at the idea of the church, and to start by looking at the word church in the Bible. The meaning is context. How is a word used in the passage it finds itself? That's what determines its meaning.

And one way that the word church is used is the collection of all true believers through the ages. That's what our confession calls the invisible church. You can see it's used in that way in Hebrews 12:22-23, where the spirit says, but you have come to Mount Zion to the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem, and to an innumerable company of angels in festive gatherings, and to the assembly — and that assembly there is the word church — to the assembly of the firstborn who are enrolled in heaven, and to God the judge of all, and to the spirits of the righteous made perfect. So the word church is used for the collection of all the saints ever saved. That's the invisible church.

Another way that the word is used is the collection of professing Christians in their children at a point of time. This is a snapshot. That's what our confession calls the visible church. It's used that way in 1 Corinthians 10:32 where Paul says, give no offense to Jews or to Greeks, or to the church of God. A church that is present right now. You can't offend a church that's not present at the moment. 1 Corinthians 15:9 says, for I am the least of the apostles, and not worthy to be called an apostle because I persecuted the church of God. The word church here, Paul was actually after physical Christians at the time he lived.

Also the church, the word church used to talk about the gathering of believers in the same place — the local church. In Colossians 4:15 it says, give my greetings to the brothers at Laodicea and to Nympha and the church in her house. So that's the church of the local church meeting at Nympha's house.

Another fourth way is that the word church is used for the collection of several churches in a geographical area. In Acts 9:31, using the ESV, it says, the church throughout all Judea and Galilee. So the word singular church is talking about all the gatherings in that area.

And then the word church is also used to refer to the church as an organism, emphasizing the people in it. That's how Paul uses it in 1 Corinthians 12 through 14, where he talks about the church as a body, as a different parts of the body there.

And lastly the word church is also used to refer to the church as an institution to which the sacraments were given, which exercises church discipline, to which corporate worship was given, which is called the ground and pillar of the church. Paul uses that way in 1 Timothy 3:15, where he says, but if I am delayed, I write so that you may know how you ought to conduct yourself in the house of God, which is the church of the living God, the pillar and ground of the truth.

It is to this use of the word church — the institutional church — that the great commission is given, to define what the church does in the great commission. And in this part of our study, this is the definition of the church that we're going to be considering.

## The Three Marks of a True Church

Using this broadly accepted definition of the church — the Belgian Confession Article 29, marks of the true church — we can identify the first time that these three elements were present in one institution. So where is the first time in the Bible where the preaching of the word, the sacraments, and church discipline was present? When we identify that, that's the beginning of the true church of Jesus Christ.

Well, the first time we find all these three elements present is in the covenant God makes with Abraham, particularly in Genesis chapter 17, which is way earlier than most of you probably grew up believing or being taught. Most of evangelicalism in the United States teaches that the church began in Acts chapter two. There are a few thousand years light in their assessment.

The church began with Abraham. Abraham is commanded in that covenant to administer the sign of the covenant to all who are eligible. Every male in his house. Romans 4 actually calls circumcision a sacrament — a sign and seal of the covenant. And what is this language of sign and seal? Is that not the language of sacrament?

Look at what it says in Genesis chapter 17, verses 9 through 13. And God said to Abraham, as for you, you shall keep my covenant, you and your offspring after you throughout their generations. This is my covenant which you shall keep between me and you and your offspring after you, every male among you shall be circumcised. You shall be circumcised in the flesh of your foreskin and it shall be a sign of the covenant between me and you. He who is eight days old among you shall be circumcised. Every male throughout your generations whether born in your house or bought with your money from any foreigner who is not of your offspring, both he who is born in your house and he who is bought with your money shall surely be circumcised so that my covenant be in your flesh as an everlasting covenant.

So what is God saying? Here Abraham, you are to faithfully practice the sacraments, in this case circumcision. Look at that, the first mark, one of the marks of a true church, present in the Abrahamic covenant.

But it doesn't stop there. Abraham was to cut off from his household anyone who refused the proper administration of the sacrament of circumcision. And in verse 14 of Genesis chapter 17, it continues and God says, any uncircumcised male who is not circumcised in the flesh of his foreskin shall be cut off from his people. He has broken my covenant. The idea here is that Abraham is to circumcise every male in his household. And if a male refuses the proper administration of the sacrament, he is to be cut off. Now, that means put out of the household, put out of that people. And God likes a little bit of irony — if he doesn't want to be cut with the sacrament, cut him off. That's what God is saying here. So here, faithfully administered sacraments, faithfully practiced church discipline.

Now, are we missing? Preaching, right? Abraham then faithfully proclaimed the word of the covenant to his household. God is speaking here in Genesis 18, verse 19, God says, for I have known him in order that he may command his children and his household after him, that they keep the way of the Lord to do righteousness and justice, that the Lord may bring to Abraham that which He has spoken to him. So what does God say? I know Abraham, he is going to proclaim the word I gave to him. He's going to make sure of his children, his household, what's going on? And you might say, but where is the gospel? Well, Jesus tells us in John 8 that Abraham knew of him and rejoiced in him. Romans 4, Paul tells us that Abraham believed the same gospel that we believe. So when he's teaching his household, he's preaching to them. He is preaching the gospel.

Look at that, all three marks of a true church, present in the Abrahamic covenant. So before Abraham, people were calling on the name of the Lord. Genesis 4 is the first time this expression is used, people calling on the name of the Lord. And we know for certain that the first person to have saving faith was Abel — the book of Hebrews explicitly tells us that Abel was a believer. But the people of God are not organized into a body and the worship of God is not regulated until God enters into covenant with Abraham. And that is the beginning of the visible church. Not Acts chapter 2, but the book of Genesis, and the very foundation of the organization of the institution of the visible church of Jesus Christ.

All right, so let's pray. Father, having thank you for your goodness to us, we thank you that you are a God who teaches through your word and through your church. We pray that we will be faithful to what your word teaches us. We pray in Jesus name, Amen.
